proportionality.description: NIS2 distinguishes essential and important entities with different supervisory regimes

tiers.name: essential
tiers.applies_to: Annex I sectors above size thresholds, plus designated entities (Art. 3(1))
tiers.note: Proactive supervision by competent authority. Higher fines (Art. 34): max EUR 10M or 2% worldwide turnover.

tiers.name: important
tiers.applies_to: Annex I/II sectors meeting medium enterprise thresholds (Art. 3(2))
tiers.note: Reactive supervision (ex post). Lower fines: max EUR 7M or 1.4% worldwide turnover.

pitfalls.description: NIS2 is a directive, not a regulation. Member states transpose into national law with variations. Always check the national implementing act via the relevant Law MCP.
Art. 21(2)(d) requires supply chain security measures including security-related aspects of relationships with direct suppliers and service providers. This extends beyond ICT vendors.

NIS2 Art. 4 recognizes lex specialis: where sector-specific EU legislation (e.g., DORA) imposes equivalent or higher cybersecurity requirements, those provisions prevail. Financial entities subject to DORA may be exempt from NIS2 cybersecurity risk management.

cross_regulation.note: DORA is lex specialis for financial entities. Where DORA addresses ICT risk, NIS2 defers.
Critical Entities Resilience Directive complements NIS2 for physical resilience of critical infrastructure.

national_implementation.pattern: NIS2 is a directive — search the relevant Law MCP for national transposition acts (deadline was 2024-10-17).

timeline.note: Member states must transpose into national law

citation_format: Cite as: NIS2 Art. X(Y). Note: NIS2 is Directive (EU) 2022/2555.
